Course Title: Using AI with Electronic Health Records (EHRs)
Category: Healthcare & Life Sciences: Medical Services, Pharmacy, Biotechnology, Hospitals
Description: Risks of data exposure and incorrect summarisation.
Prerequisites: Learners should have a basic familiarity with electronic health records systems and a general understanding of how AI tools are used in everyday workflows, though no advanced technical knowledge is required.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key risks associated with data exposure when using AI tools alongside Electronic Health Records (EHRs), and explain how these risks can affect patient confidentiality and organisational compliance. You will also be able to apply practical strategies to recognise and reduce the impact of incorrect AI summarisation within clinical and healthcare settings.

lessons.lesson title: Using AI with Electronic Health Records (EHRs)
lessons.Description: This lesson introduces healthcare professionals to the practical realities of using artificial intelligence tools alongside Electronic Health Records (EHRs). You will explore how AI can support clinical workflows, such as summarising patient notes or flagging key information, while also understanding the significant risks that come with this technology. The lesson covers how sensitive patient data can be exposed when information is shared with AI systems, and why incorrect or incomplete summaries generated by AI could lead to serious clinical errors. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of how to approach AI tools with appropriate caution, and what safeguards to consider when working with patient records.
lessons.content: Risks of data exposure and incorrect summarisation.

slides.subtitle: Risks, Rewards, and Responsible Use
slides.slidecontent:
• AI tools are increasingly being used alongside Electronic Health Records in clinical settings
• Understanding the risks matters
• Incorrect summarisation and data exposure are two of the most pressing concerns for healthcare staff
• This lesson covers both the potential benefits and the responsibilities that come with using AI in patient care
• No prior technical knowledge needed

slides.subtitle: What could go wrong with AI?
slides.slidecontent:
• AI can misread or misrepresent patient information, leading to incorrect summaries in the record
• Sensitive data may be exposed if AI tools are not properly secured
• Errors in AI output can go unnoticed if clinicians trust results without checking
• Data privacy risks
• AI may reflect biases present in the data it was trained on, producing unfair or inaccurate outputs for some patient groups
• Audit trails can become unclear when AI contributes to clinical documentation

slides.subtitle: What could go wrong with AI?
slides.slidecontent:
• Data can be exposed accidentally
• AI may summarise records incorrectly
• Patient safety risks arise
• Errors can go unnoticed
• Bias affects AI outputs

slides.subtitle: When AI Gets the Summary Wrong
slides.slidecontent:
• Wrong drugs listed
• Missed allergies
• Incorrect diagnoses recorded
• Outdated information shown
• Patient safety at risk

slides.subtitle: Practical steps to protect patient data
slides.slidecontent:
• Always use NHS-approved or trust-approved AI tools only
• Never paste identifiable patient data into consumer AI applications
• Double-check every AI-generated summary before acting on it
• Errors can be subtle
• Report any suspected data breach immediately using your organisation's standard incident process
• Keep a brief record of when and how AI was used in any clinical task

slides.subtitle: Practical steps to protect patient data
slides.slidecontent:
• Always use NHS-approved or trust-approved AI tools only
• Never paste identifiable patient data into consumer AI applications
• Double-check every AI-generated summary before acting on it
• Errors happen
• Report any suspected data breaches to your information governance team immediately
• Keep a brief record of when and how AI was used in your clinical workflow

slides.subtitle: Safeguards every clinician should implement
slides.slidecontent:
• Always verify AI summaries
• Check patient details carefully
• Flag unexpected AI outputs
• Never skip manual review
• Report errors promptly

slides.subtitle: Protect data, verify AI outputs always.
slides.slidecontent:
• Never share patient data carelessly
• Always verify AI summaries
• Errors cause real patient harm
• Report concerns to your supervisor
• Stay curious, keep learning

scripts.script: Welcome to this lesson on using AI with Electronic Health Records. AI tools are becoming a regular part of clinical life, and many of you will already be encountering them in your day-to-day work, whether you realise it or not. But as with any new technology in healthcare, it is not enough to simply know how to use these tools. We also need to understand what can go wrong. Two of the biggest concerns for healthcare staff right now are AI getting things wrong when summarising patient information, and the risk of sensitive data being exposed in ways we might not expect. Throughout this lesson, we will look honestly at both sides: the genuine benefits AI can bring to patient care, and the very real responsibilities that come with it. And do not worry if you are not particularly technical. No specialist knowledge is required here. Just an open mind and a willingness to think carefully about how we use these tools responsibly.

Before we look at how AI can help us work with electronic health records, it's really important that we're honest about the risks involved. AI tools can make mistakes, and in a clinical setting, those mistakes can have serious consequences. For example, AI might misread or misrepresent patient information, which could lead to errors in the record. There are also real concerns around data privacy, particularly if the tools being used aren't properly secured. One of the biggest dangers is over-reliance. If clinicians simply accept what the AI produces without checking it, errors can slip through unnoticed. It's also worth knowing that AI systems can carry biases from the data they were trained on, which means outputs might be less accurate or even unfair for certain patient groups. And finally, when AI contributes to clinical documentation, it can make audit trails harder to follow. So as we go through this course, keeping these risks in mind is absolutely essential.

So we've looked at what AI can do with electronic health records, but now let's be honest about the risks, because there are some real concerns we need to talk about. First, patient data can be accidentally exposed if AI tools aren't properly secured or configured, and that's a serious confidentiality issue. Beyond that, AI doesn't always get things right. It might summarise a patient's record incorrectly, and if a clinician acts on that without checking, you can see how quickly that becomes a patient safety problem. What makes this particularly tricky is that errors can slip through unnoticed, especially when people start to trust the technology too much and reduce their own scrutiny. And there's another layer to this as well: AI systems can carry bias. If the data they were trained on wasn't representative, the outputs may disadvantage certain groups of patients. So while AI offers genuine benefits, we always need to approach it with a critical eye.

Now, let's talk about what can go wrong when AI summarises a patient's record incorrectly, because the consequences here are genuinely serious. Imagine a clinician glancing at an AI-generated summary and seeing the wrong medication listed, or not seeing a critical allergy flagged at all. That kind of error could lead directly to a patient being harmed. It is not just about drugs and allergies either. If the AI records an incorrect diagnosis, or pulls through information that is months or even years out of date, the clinical picture becomes distorted. Decisions get made based on a version of the patient that no longer reflects reality. This is why AI summarisation in healthcare is not something we can treat casually. The technology can be incredibly useful, but it needs proper oversight. Someone with clinical knowledge must always review what the AI produces, because when it gets things wrong in this setting, patient safety is genuinely on the line.

So let's talk about how you can actually protect yourself and your patients when using AI day to day. The golden rule is straightforward: only ever use tools that have been approved by your NHS trust or organisation. That means no copying patient details into general consumer apps like ChatGPT or similar. Those tools are not designed for clinical data, and doing so could constitute a serious data breach. When AI does produce a summary or suggestion, always review it carefully before you act on it. Errors can be easy to miss because the output often looks polished and confident, even when something is subtly wrong. If you do suspect a breach has occurred, report it straight away through your organisation's usual incident reporting process. And finally, get into the habit of making a brief note of when and how you used AI in a clinical task. It keeps things transparent and protects you professionally.

So, let's talk about how you can actually protect yourself and your patients when using AI day to day. The golden rule is simple: only ever use tools that have been approved by your NHS trust or organisation. That means avoiding the temptation to copy and paste patient details into general consumer AI tools like ChatGPT, because those platforms are not designed for confidential clinical data. When AI does produce a summary or suggestion, always review it carefully before you act on it, because these tools do make mistakes, and the responsibility for clinical decisions remains with you. If something does go wrong and you suspect a data breach has occurred, report it to your information governance team straight away, do not delay. And finally, get into the habit of making a brief note in your workflow of when and how you used AI. It does not need to be lengthy, just enough to keep things transparent and accountable.

So, AI can be incredibly helpful when working with electronic health records, but it is not infallible, and protecting your patients from potential errors is absolutely essential. The first thing to get into the habit of is always checking any summary or suggestion the AI produces. Never simply accept it at face value. Take a moment to verify that the patient details are correct, because even a small mix-up in names, dates, or medication information could have serious consequences. If something looks unusual or unexpected in the AI output, do not ignore it. Flag it straight away and investigate further. It can be tempting, especially when you are busy, to skip the manual review stage, but please do not. That human check is your safety net. And finally, if you do spot an error, report it promptly through the appropriate channels. This helps your organisation learn, improve the system, and ultimately keep every patient safer.

So before we wrap up this lesson, let's bring together the most important things to hold onto. First and foremost, patient data is sensitive and must always be handled with care. Never share it casually or beyond what is necessary. When AI produces a summary or a recommendation, do not simply accept it at face value. Always check it yourself, because mistakes in a clinical setting are not just inconvenient, they can genuinely harm patients. If something does not look right, or if you have concerns about how AI is being used in your workplace, please speak to your supervisor. You are not expected to have all the answers, but you are expected to raise questions when something feels off. And finally, this field is moving quickly, so stay curious and keep building your knowledge over time. The more you understand about these tools, the better placed you will be to use them safely and responsibly.

Who is this for: Healthcare professionals, nurses, physicians, medical assistants, clinical staff, healthcare administrators, health information managers, medical records clerks, healthcare IT users
Team or Dept this is for: Clinical Operations, Health Information Management, Healthcare IT, Medical Records, Nursing, Quality Assurance, Compliance, Risk Management
